title: Batch Processing: Serverless / Pre-Warmed Workers
description: Batch job execution using serverless functions or pre-warmed worker pools (AWS Lambda, Cloud Run Jobs, AWS Fargate). Achieves the fastest job start times (P95 ≤ 10s) by eliminating cold-start overhead through pre-provisioned execution environments. Best for latency-sensitive batch workloads, event-driven batch jobs, and workloads with unpredictable arrival patterns. Higher per-invocation cost compared to cluster-based batch, but eliminates idle infrastructure costs. Maximum job duration typically 15-60 minutes depending on provider.

cost.costNotes: Serverless batch pricing is pure pay-per-invocation with no idle costs. Min ($20): low-volume batch jobs on Lambda or Cloud Run Jobs with free tier coverage. Max ($3000): high-frequency serverless batch with pre-warmed pools and significant data transfer.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Serverless/pre-warmed batch has no idle infrastructure cost but higher per-invocation cost. Min ($20): light batch workloads on Lambda free tier + S3 storage costs. Max ($3000): high-frequency pre-warmed Cloud Run Jobs or Fargate at ~$0.04/vCPU-hour with 100k+ monthly invocations and data egress.
provenance.adoptionCost: Slightly higher than base batch due to serverless-specific considerations: function packaging limits, timeout constraints (Lambda 15min, Cloud Run Jobs 24hr), pre-warming configuration, and stateless execution design. Estimate 13-14 developer days at ~$180/day = $2500.
provenance.licenseCost: No license cost. All major serverless batch platforms (Lambda, Cloud Run Jobs, Fargate) are usage-based with no upfront licensing.

provides.reasoning: Provides batch processing capability via serverless execution — processing data in triggered invocations rather than long-running cluster processes.
Enables ETL batch workflows with fast job start and per-invocation billing suited to periodic data processing.
Serverless batch achieves high throughput through massive horizontal scaling — thousands of concurrent function invocations with sub-second scheduling.

requires.reasoning: Serverless batch jobs are typically triggered by event sources (S3 events, EventBridge schedules, SQS) acting as the scheduler.
Orchestration required to coordinate multi-step serverless batch workflows (AWS Step Functions, Google Cloud Workflows).
Serverless functions must read input from and write output to persistent storage (S3, GCS, Blob Storage) due to stateless execution model.
Serverless platforms provide built-in retry with configurable backoff for transient failures in batch invocations.

supports_nfr.reason: Serverless batch is compatible with any auth mechanism; job-level auth is handled by IAM roles and execution credentials.
Serverless function execution logs provide an audit trail; compatible with both audited and non-audited environments.
Serverless batch supports tenant isolation through separate function invocations per tenant with IAM-scoped execution roles.
Serverless batch can achieve up to 99.99% availability; five-nine availability requires active-active multi-region topology.
Serverless/pre-warmed workers achieve P95 job start in 1-10 seconds. Pre-warmed Lambda or Cloud Run Jobs eliminate cold-start overhead, enabling sub-10s P95 start times.
Serverless batch optimizes for fast job start (P95 5min). For jobs tolerating >5min start latency, cluster-based batch is more cost-effective.
P99 job start for serverless/pre-warmed workers is 10-30 seconds, accounting for occasional cold starts under high concurrency or after idle periods.
Serverless batch RTO is 5-15 minutes: fast invocation restart but requires time to detect failure, re-queue, and re-invoke from last checkpoint.
Serverless batch RPO can be as low as 1 minute with fine-grained checkpointing to durable storage between function invocations.
Serverless batch can handle up to 360M jobs/hour through massive horizontal scaling of function invocations.
At <10 jobs/hour, serverless per-invocation costs and orchestration overhead outweigh benefits compared to simpler cron-based execution.
Serverless batch is compatible with PII workloads when execution environments are configured with appropriate encryption and IAM controls.
Serverless batch is compatible with GDPR through proper data handling, deletion capabilities, and ephemeral execution environments.
Serverless batch supports HIPAA compliance with encryption at rest/transit, BAA coverage, and appropriate access controls.
Serverless batch is compatible with CCPA when configured to support consumer data deletion within required timeframes.
Serverless batch supports SOX compliance through function execution logs providing an immutable audit trail.
Serverless batch is stateless and eventually consistent across invocations. Read-your-writes requires compensating patterns and complicates job design.
Strict durability achievable with idempotent invocations and checkpointing to durable storage between function executions.

supports_constraints.reason: Serverless batch is available on all major clouds (Lambda, Cloud Run Jobs, Azure Container Apps Jobs) and can be emulated on-prem with Knative.
Serverless batch supports all major languages via containerized functions or native runtimes.
Serverless batch targets scheduled job execution platforms; api platform excluded as batch is not a request-response architecture.
Serverless batch works with or without caching; in-memory caching is limited by function lifetime but external caches work normally.
Serverless batch commonly uses SQS/Pub-Sub as event sources but can also run on scheduled triggers without messaging.
Serverless batch supports AI inference workloads within function timeout limits; suitable for embedding generation and batch predictions.
Serverless batch supports multi-tenant workloads through per-tenant function invocations with separate IAM roles.
Pattern requires batch_processing feature flag — this is the serverless variant of the batch processing implementation pattern.
Serverless batch can coordinate distributed transactions using saga patterns but stateless execution complicates transaction management.
Serverless batch is compatible with lambda architecture combining serverless batch and real-time streaming.
Serverless batch can generate embeddings and build vector indexes in batch invocations.
Serverless batch works with document stores for reading source data and writing batch results.
Serverless batch can use key-value stores for job state and deduplication across invocations.
Serverless batch supports graph analytics and bulk graph mutation jobs within function duration limits.
Serverless batch handles time-series aggregation and historical rollup jobs.
Serverless batch can execute bulk OLTP operations during off-peak windows.
Serverless batch naturally supports scheduled OLAP aggregation jobs.

worker_type.description: Execution environment. Serverless: AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Run Jobs, Azure Container Apps Jobs with pre-warmed execution environments.

schedule_type.description: How batches are triggered. Event_driven is most common for serverless batch (SQS, S3 events, EventBridge).

retry_strategy.description: How failed invocations are retried. Exponential_backoff recommended for serverless to avoid thundering herd.

parallelism.description: Execution model. Partitioned recommended for serverless: split input into chunks, each chunk processed by separate function invocation.

failure_handling.description: What happens when individual records fail. Dead_letter_queue recommended for serverless to capture failed invocations for reprocessing.

warn_nfr.reason: Serverless batch with availability target <95% indicates non-critical workload; simpler cron-based solutions may be more cost-effective than managed batch infrastructure.
warn_nfr.message: data-batch-processing--serverless: availability target is {actual} (<95%). For non-critical batch jobs, simpler cron-based execution may reduce operational overhead.
